review_text:
[b]Jumping into Counter-Strike 2 in 2026 is like revisiting a famously strict boarding school that recently got a beautiful visual overhaul but kept all of its most terrifying disciplinarians. Built on the gorgeous Source 2 engine, the game is visually stunning, featuring dynamic, volumetric smoke grenades that practically deserve their own casting credits and pristine lighting that perfectly illuminates every poor tactical decision you make. Yet, beneath the shiny new coat of paint, it remains the exact same ruthless, hyper-competitive trial by fire that has been systematically humbling gamers for well over two decades. There is absolutely no hand-holding here, your survival depends entirely on map knowledge, crisp crosshair placement, and the psychological fortitude to handle teammates who expect you to play like a seasoned esports professional in a casual Tuesday night lobby.

The core gameplay loop remains an addictive masterpiece of tension and release, but Valve continuously tweaks the formula to ensure nobody gets too comfortable. Recent updates have thrown a massive wrench into the muscle memory of veterans by introducing a realistic magazine reloading mechanic, meaning that if you habitually reload after firing a single warning shot, you will now unceremoniously throw the rest of your precious bullets directly into the trash. It is a hilarious and brutal way to force better ammo management, leading to countless moments of sheer panic as players realize their guns are completely empty during a crucial push. Combine that with the highly debated sub-tick server architecture and newly refined movement mechanics, and you have a tactical shooter that demands absolute precision while actively laughing at your attempts to master it.

As a free-to-play juggernaut, the game undeniably carries the classic baggage of massive online titles, from a wildly volatile matchmaking experience to an ongoing, exhausting arms race against cheaters. The learning curve is practically a vertical cliff, and you will spend your first few dozen hours spectating other players while trying to figure out why standing slightly to the left got you instantly eliminated through a wall. However, if you can push through the initial frustration, learn to ignore the occasionally toxic team communications, and embrace the sheer dopamine rush of landing a perfect one-tap headshot, you will quickly understand why millions of people absolutely refuse to play anything else. It is a brilliant, agonizing, beautifully updated playground that will consistently make you swear you are uninstalling forever, right before you immediately queue up for just one more match.[/b]

review_text:
Counter-Strike 2 feels like Valve took a legendary game, put it through a washing machine, and forgot to check if anything still works afterward.

Sure, the smoke physics are fancy now—great, I can’t see anything in higher resolution. It’s like being blinded, but in 4K. Every match is a coin flip between a tactical masterpiece and five people running around like they just discovered the keyboard yesterday.

The servers? Amazing. I love getting shot after I’ve already taken cover. It really adds that “time travel” mechanic no one asked for.

And then there’s playing with my friend Toivo105. Watching him play is like observing a scientific study on how many wrong decisions a human can make in 30 seconds. He moves like he’s permanently surprised, buys weapons like he’s shopping blindfolded, and somehow turns every round into a mystery novel where the main question is: “How did it go this wrong?” You don’t get mad—you just sit there, impressed in a deeply concerned way.

The ranking system also deserves an award for confusion. I’ve seen better organization in a sock drawer during an earthquake.

Yet somehow, despite the chaos, the bugs, and the emotional damage, I keep coming back. At this point, I’m not playing to win—I’m playing to see what new and creative way the game will disappoint me next.

10/10 would uninstall and reinstall again at 3AM.

review_text:
Listen up, you pixel-pushing amateurs.

I’ve seen a lot of battlefields in my time, but Counter-Strike 2? That’s a proper warzone. Tight corners, no-nonsense gunplay, and if you screw up, you’re done—just like it should be. None of that hand-holding, respawn-every-five-seconds garbage. You earn your wins here.

The weapons hit hard, the smoke actually behaves like it’s got a brain, and every round feels like it matters. You want glory? You clutch it. You want respect? You take it. Simple.

And the teamwork? Don’t even think about going lone wolf unless you’ve got the skills to back it up. This game rewards discipline, precision, and guts. Reminds me of the old days… except now the explosions are cleaner and nobody’s actually bleeding. Shame.

Bottom line: CS2 doesn’t care about your feelings. It’s brutal, it’s sharp, and it separates the pros from the wannabes.

Now grab a rifle and prove you belong.

review_text:
Counter-Strike 2 to dla wielu po prostu kolejna odsłona kultowej serii FPS — poprawiona grafika, lepsza fizyka dymu, bardziej responsywny gameplay. Ale dla mnie ta gra stała się czymś znacznie więcej niż tylko strzelanką.

Zaczęło się całkiem zwyczajnie. Kolejny matchmaking, losowy team, standardowe „gl hf” na czacie. I wtedy pojawił się on. Na początku tylko głos na voice chacie — spokojny, trochę ironiczny, ale jednocześnie niesamowicie ciepły. Zagraliśmy razem kilka rund, potem kolejne mecze. Z czasem przestaliśmy rozmawiać tylko o taktykach i fragach.

CS2 ma w sobie coś wyjątkowego — zmusza ludzi do współpracy, do komunikacji, do zaufania. W naszym przypadku przerodziło się to w coś znacznie głębszego. Zaczęliśmy grać codziennie, potem pisać poza grą, aż w końcu rozmowy trwały dłużej niż same mecze.

Gameplay? Świetny. Nowy silnik robi robotę, smoki dynamicznie zmieniają sytuację na mapie, a feeling strzelania jest bardziej satysfakcjonujący niż kiedykolwiek. Ale to nie dlatego wracam do tej gry każdego dnia.

Wracam, bo tam go poznałam/poznałem.

Dziś jesteśmy razem i szczerze — żadna ranga, żaden skin ani nawet najlepszy ace nie daje takiej satysfakcji jak świadomość, że przypadkowy matchmaking może zmienić całe życie.

Czy CS2 jest dobrą grą? Tak.

Czy może połączyć ludzi? Zdecydowanie.

A czy polecam? Jeśli jesteś gotowy nie tylko na headshoty, ale i na to, że ktoś może trafić prosto w Twoje serce — to zdecydowanie tak.

I don't get it, somehow they managed to make me hate their ranked system even more. 
Some content also kind of went up in flames, Danger Zone is gone (Didn't play it more than about 10 matches but its sad to see content removed during the transition to CS2), Cobblestone, one of my favorite maps is also gone from the Competitive map pool. 
Theres an Armory Pass which looks predatory, you can hold up to 5 passes at once to farm them at the same time which is nice BUT each pass is around 15 euros, five passes would cost 75 bucks and youre not guaranteed to make that money back because the skins have quality so if you get Factory New skins such as the current Limited Edition skin (AK-47 Aphrodite) youd be set (Current value at this time is 500 for the Factory New) but all other qualities dont return those 75 bucks spent, also the limited Edition is 125 credits/token and each pass gives you 40 so 3 passes only gives you 120 so you need to spend an extra 15 bucks for a fourth pass just for those 5 extra tokens youre missing if you want the LE Skin, the leftovers can be spent opening boxes and hoping you get something good, you would basically gamble 60 bucks in hopes of making profit, this is quite possibly one of the worst and most predatory Pass systems ive ever seen. 
As for cheaters I haven't found as many in CS2 as I did in CSGO so either that got better or I got lucky. 
The firing/bullet system, I kind of always disliked it in this game, how ive seen people hit shots with an AK by spraying at the ground, not their feet, the ground in front of them and it hits their torso, its both a skill issue on my part and the absudity of it, I get that its not supposed to be realistic but that doesnt make its any less dumb, even after learning to use the AK and M4A1-S properly, controlling the recoil and bullet spread I still dont enjoy it, on FPS games I prefer to focus on fighting my enemies, not fighting enemies AND the guns, even when I land a random one tap headshot without aiming at the head it feels more confusing and lucky then satisfying but this is more of a preference thing than an issue, most will probably enjoy getting better with the weapons, gameplay seems to be the same for CS2 apart from the Smoke grenade which behaves differently and looks pretty cool. 
The game also becoming F2P (This was before CS2 I believe) made it worse, before you would buy the game and then farm for the Prime Status however when it became F2P they made it so you buy the Prime Status (Currently its 13,29 euros), to me this was one of their worst decisions, if someone cheated and got VAC banned they would need to make a new account and buy the game all over again but it being F2P means they could just make a new account and play for free and bully people without Prime, they essentially almost force people to buy prime to avoid cheaters as I assume without Prime there are a lot more cheaters than before, with Prime maybe there is less now since Cheaters dont need to buy Prime, they can just bully people in the non-prime matches because its 100% free, they arent forced to re-buy the game every time theyget banned so maybe Prime matches have less cheaters but its kind of a f*ck you to people who dont have/want to spend almost 14 bucks for a simple Status, at least before you were paying for the game itself, now youre paying for a higher chance to avoid cheaters. 

People will either not care about these issues due to having low standards, not interacting with the predatory systems and/or because they are long time players who invested so much time playing the game, gettings skins and getting good that they feel the need to lick the game's n*ts to justify all the time and/or money they spent in this game but people with common sense will realize this game got worse over time, now it looks pretty but thats it, just a fresh coat of paint to hide the bad changes.

review_text:
🔥 10/10 would get tilted again 🔥

I’ve been playing Counter-Strike 2 (and since the good old 1.6 days), and let me tell you… this game is not just a game — it’s a lifestyle, a mental test, and sometimes a full-blown emotional rollercoaster 😤

You queue up thinking, “Just one game.”
Next thing you know, it’s 3AM, your aim is either god-tier or completely gone, and you’re debating life choices because you whiffed a full mag on someone not even looking at you.

🎯 Gameplay

Simple on the surface: shoot heads, plant bomb, defuse bomb.
But in reality? It’s chess with guns.

One round you’re hitting crispy one-taps like a pro
Next round you forget how to counter-strafe and die to a guy named “toaster”
Utility? Either perfectly executed… or you just flash your entire team 💀
🧠 Game Sense & Skill

This game will humble you FAST.

You’ll think you’re improving…
Then someone wide swings you at Mach 10 and deletes you before your brain processes what happened.

But that’s the beauty of it:

You actually improve over time
Every loss teaches you something (even if it’s painful)
When everything clicks? It feels AMAZING
🤝 Teammates (the real gamble)

You don’t queue for a match. You queue for an experience.

You might get:

A chill squad with perfect comms 🤝
A silent team playing like it’s a single-player game 🎧
Or that one guy screaming into his mic like it’s a championship final 🎤💀

But somehow… you keep coming back.

🔫 Gunplay

Still one of the BEST in any FPS.

No aim assist saving you here
Movement matters
Crosshair placement is life
Spray control = skill gap

When you land that clean headshot? Pure dopamine.

😤 The Struggle
Missed easy shots
Getting one-tapped instantly
Losing eco rounds you should win
“He’s lit 99…” (he wasn’t)

But somehow… you hit “Play Again” every time.

🏆 Final Thoughts

This game will:

Tilt you
Test you
Improve you
And keep you addicted for YEARS

Not perfect. Not easy.
But when you hit those clean plays and clutch rounds… there’s NOTHING like it.

Verdict:
⭐ Addicting
⭐ Competitive
⭐ Painful
⭐ Beautiful

review_text:
Counter-Strike 2 is an exceptionally intense and meticulously crafted competitive shooter that transforms a simple concept into a deeply strategic, adrenaline-fueled experience. At first glance, the game may appear straightforward: two teams compete through rounds of tactical combat, precision shooting, and objective-based gameplay. However, beneath that surface lies an extraordinarily intricate system of mechanical skill, communication, positioning, map knowledge, and psychological decision-making.

One of the most impressive aspects of Counter-Strike 2 is its commitment to competitive purity. Every movement, every angle, every grenade throw, and every shot carries immense significance. Unlike many modern shooters that rely heavily on flashy abilities or overwhelming visual chaos, CS2 places the player’s individual skill and teamwork at the very center of the experience. This creates a game that feels both unforgiving and deeply rewarding. Success is not handed to the player; it must be earned through patience, discipline, and constant improvement.

The visual upgrade from previous Counter-Strike titles is immediately noticeable. The lighting, smoke effects, weapon models, and redesigned maps give the game a much more polished and contemporary appearance. The new smoke mechanics are especially impressive, as they add a dynamic layer to strategy. Smokes now interact with bullets and grenades, making utility usage feel more realistic and consequential. This change enhances the tactical depth of each round and forces players to adapt quickly to shifting situations.

The gunplay remains the heart of the game, and it is remarkably satisfying. Weapons feel powerful, precise, and demanding. Mastering recoil patterns, crosshair placement, and timing is essential, and the game rewards players who are willing to invest the time to refine their mechanics. There is a certain elegance in how CS2 refuses to overcomplicate its core gameplay. It understands exactly what makes Counter-Strike iconic and preserves that identity while modernizing the overall presentation.

That being said, Counter-Strike 2 can also be extremely challenging for new players. The learning curve is steep, and beginners may initially feel overwhelmed by experienced opponents, complex map callouts, economy management, and the importance of utility usage. The game does not always explain its systems clearly, which can make the early experience intimidating. However, for players who are persistent, this difficulty becomes part of the appeal. Improvement feels tangible, and every successful clutch, headshot, or coordinated execute feels genuinely gratifying.

Overall, Counter-Strike 2 is a sophisticated, high-pressure, and brilliantly competitive shooter. It may not be the most casual or forgiving game, but it excels at what it sets out to be: a precise, tactical, and skill-driven experience. For players who enjoy competition, strategy, and the pursuit of mastery, CS2 is an outstanding game that continues to prove why Counter-Strike remains one of the most influential franchises in gaming history.

review_text:
I downloaded Counter-Strike 2 and said, “This time I’m gonna play seriously.” First round: I throw a flash… the only person blinded is me. My teammate asks, “Why did you flash yourself?” I said it’s strategy. He goes, “Psychological?” He might be right.

The game is very realistic. For example, you hear footsteps… but figuring out where they’re coming from requires you to be Sherlock Holmes. Every time I hear a sound, I spin 360 degrees. Result: I get dizzy and die anyway.

The smokes are new technology and all. Nice, cool… but when I walk into a smoke, I feel like I’m lost on a foggy village road in the morning. Someone comes out of it, they see me, I don’t see them. Apparently, the smoke doesn’t work for me.

The economy system is amazing too. Lose two rounds and it’s instantly “let’s eco.” When we eco, the enemy team looks like they’re playing with a NASA budget. We’ve got Glocks and hope; they’ve got full gear and confidence.

Aim is a whole different story. My crosshair is right on the guy’s head, I click… nothing happens. Then he turns around and one-taps me. There’s a hidden feature in this game: “luck.” I don’t have that DLC.

But no matter what, you can’t quit the game. You get tilted, you say “that’s enough”… and two minutes later you’re back in queue. CS2 isn’t just a game, it’s a lifestyle. A bit of pain, a bit of chaos, and a lot of “one last match.”

review_text:
This game genuinely changed my life.

I’m 29.

My ex and I share a daughter, and we also adopted a son together. They’re both 7 now.

When everything started falling apart between us, I didn’t handle it well. I felt completely lost. Angry, numb, reckless. One night, I stepped outside my front door, lost it, and kicked the brick wall as hard as I could.

Worst decision of my life.

I ended up shattering three metatarsals and eight phalanges in my foot. My ability to work… gone. My independence… gone. I couldn’t even move around without pain, let alone walk with my kids to support the local empanada store. Sorry Juan…

Hearing the diagnosis broke something in me. I sank hard into depression. Anxiety got worse. Days blurred together. I stopped enjoying anything. I’ve never been the type to act out like that, but I was at such a low point that I just didn’t see a way forward.

With nothing but time and a lack of local Mexican cuisine, I started searching for something, anything, to take my mind off it. I needed something I could do sitting down, something simple.

That’s when I stumbled across this game.

I wasn’t convinced at first. I read through reviews, hesitated a bit… but I figured I had nothing to lose. So I downloaded it.

And I’m glad I did.

After a while, something shifted. For the first time in months, I felt… okay. Then better than okay. I had something to focus on. Something to build. Something that felt like progress. The weight I’d been carrying started to lift.

At one point, I just sat there for a few minutes, listening to the music.

That’s when it clicked. Uber Eats.

I shut the game off, went downstairs, and waited. My mouth began to salivate as I waited for my order to arrive.

And in that moment, I stopped feeling like a victim. I remembered who I needed to be for my kids, not the version of me that gives up, but the one who shows up no matter what.

Been an Uber One member ever since.

Highly recommended for anyone who has too much peace of mind and wants to lose it. 10/10, see you on the server.

review_text:
👍 Recommended

CS2 is a fantastic free-to-play experience where 10 people join a server and only 2 of them are actually playing the same game.

The gunplay feels crisp, movement is smooth, and every match offers a surprise mechanic called **“Was that skill or software?”**

Premier mode is especially exciting because your rank depends on:
• aim
• teamwork
• luck
• whether the spinning scout on the enemy team gets tired

VAC remains one of gaming’s most beloved decorative features. It adds a comforting sense of tradition without disrupting anyone’s evening.

Still, nothing matches the thrill of hitting a clean headshot, clutching a 1v3, or convincing yourself the enemy was just “having a good day.”

Would recommend to:
✔ people who enjoy competition
✔ people who enjoy suffering
✔ people who enjoy both simultaneously

10/10 Stockholm syndrome simulator.

3/5 Stars

The game is still there and its okay. Valve should've learned from Back 4 Blood and realised their OG games will be hard to top. CS2 is good but ask players if they would rather play CS2 or CS:GO and I promise the majority would rather play CS:GO.

Current Issues with the game:
-Movement feels floaty and slow (especially compared to CS:GO)
-Weapon Accuracy isnt as good as GO, running and gunning is more promoted
-Delay before throwing nades is slower and more "clunky"
-Peekers-advantage is still more prominent in CS2 than GO
-The AWP is overall worse than GO
-lack of feed back i.e csgo blood came from the bullet holes, cs2 just sprays out the player model sometimes, meaning shots with snipers are hard to tell if u hit them

Theres more and each issue have more that can be said. Valve should have left CS2 in a public access with CS:GO still active. Its taken over 2 and half years for CS2 to even start to feel close to CS:GO.
Secure VAC and 128 tick would've kept 90% of the community happy on CS:GO for years to come, instead we are forced to play this and its driving die hard fans away.

review_text:
This game seems cool. The item system is pretty in-depth and the heroes have very interesting kits. I'd love to get into this game. However...
This game's new player experience is probably only marginally better than waterboarding. The game forces you to play a bunch of ♥♥♥♥♥♥ bot matches before letting you play against real players. This is okay for most players and not something I have an issue with. The real issue is what comes after: You have to play 100 hours of the unranked mode before you're allowed to play ranked. This makes sense, right? It makes smurfing less prevalent and it stops clueless players from playing ranked. But the real issue isn't this measure, it's the absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ godawful unranked matchmaking.
I made another attempt to get into the game today and I had 2 reasonably balanced matches before being dumped into three consecutive matches where the enemy team has a collective 20k+ games played, while mine has maybe 200 in total. These were obviously complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Hilariously enough, all of these were matches where you could swap one or two player's teams, and have a much more balanced experience. This is a calculation that would take a computer maybe a few nanoseconds to make. The matchmaker, seemingly, does not make this calculation. The result? Absolute misery for 5 people for 25+ minutes. And this just keeps happening. This is not an isolated issue either. I looked online and other new players deal with the same exact thing. 
I'm sure there'll be some ignorant ♥♥♥♥ in the comments saying "Oh well. This game is hard. That's just how it is. It's just a skill issue on your part. Go play an easier game". What I have to say to you is: I've played every other MOBA under the sun. I've played RTS games, I've played strategy games, I've played fast paced shooters. I'm fine with games that need a PhD to understand. I'm willing to work through being bad and to get a PhD of my own. The problem, however, is that I'm in ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ kindergarten fighting all these people that have 7 PhDs. It is not fun, it is not fair, it is not particularly educational.
♥♥♥♥ this game.

review_text:
I’ve been playing since Warcraft III back in 2006, and I moved to Dota 2 in 2014. I stopped in 2024 and only came back last month but coming back made me realize something I’ve never really said out loud.

When everything in my life starts falling apart, Dota is the one thing that’s always there.

It sounds dramatic for a game, but it’s the truth. There were moments where I didn’t know what to do, where things felt heavy, confusing, or just empty and somehow, queuing up for a match gave me something to hold onto. A reason to stay focused. A way to get through another day.

It’s not perfect. It can be frustrating, toxic, exhausting. But even then, it never left. It gave me structure when I had none, something familiar when everything else felt uncertain.

I’m still here, typing this, because I had something to come back to.

So yeah this isn’t just a game to me. It’s been a constant in a life that hasn’t always been stable.

Thank you for that.

I’m writing this with tears in my eyes and I mean every word.

review_text:
Just started playing DOTA 2 for the first time the other day, so keep in mind this is a new player's review

Just to get it out of the way, I like the game's mechanics and design, all about the game aspect here is great as far as I'm concerned, outside of some outdated models and such, that is just an aesthetic thing.

But the main issue and the reason this review is negative, is because the game is ready to waste 40 minutes of your day with no surrender option, just because someone is smurfing on the enemy team or someone on the enemy team has played five times the matches you played.

This happens almost literally every single match. And it seems the only way to enjoy the game is to either get lucky with matchmaking, or somehow magically git good and play at pro level, using matches you are getting stomped at to practice.

review_text:
Dota 2 (Дота 2, в своих кругах просто DotA (Дота), также известна как дотка, дотан, задрота, укр. гидота) — крайне популярная в настоящее время видеоигра, родоначальник жанра MOBA, основоположник соответствующих принципов и понятий. Известна в частности огромным количеством школьников, которые ее населяют, так как игра полностью бесплатная и соответственно позволить себе её может каждый, а также высокой агрессивностью игроков — так как игра командная в формате 5 на 5, многие заднюю боль от поражения приписывают команде, любят оскорблять тех, кто играет как-то не так — школьники же, себя считают мощными нагибаторами.

Нежданная реинкарнация той самой легендарной карты в Warcraft III, но уже в виде отдельной самостоятельной игры от Valve и без связи с Blizzard (позаимствовали идею и даже не заплатили аки владычные чады); многопользовательская командная видео игра жанра MOBA (стратегия в реальном времени с элементами ролевой игры), рядом с которой ни одна девушка не выдерживает конкуренции и идёт, куда подальше, ибо Дота, поистине, великолепна, она лучше всех ♥♥♥♥ на свете, и только она достойна настоящей любви.

10-го июля 2013 года любимая всеми Dota 2 официально вышла в свет во всей красе, но ещё до релиза мгновенно стала самой популярной игрой в Steam.

Несмотря на, казалось бы, всеобщую любовь к Доте, всё же находятся отщепенцы не питающие к ней большой симпатии (Еретики! Сжечь их на костре!). Эти нелюди считают Доту самой тупой игрой созданной во вселенной. На самом-то деле все знают, что Дота заслуживает даже большей любви, чем родная мать, и это чистая правда. Ведь Дота — это Пудж, и его обслуживают подпуджевики.

The visuals, sound design, and overall polish are top-tier, and the fact that it’s completely free-to-play without pay-to-win mechanics makes it even better.

It’s not just a game—it’s a long-term challenge that keeps pushing you to improve.

Highly recommended if you’re ready to invest time and effort.

review_text:
I’ve spent thousands of hours in League,  stepping into Dota feels like taking the training wheels taken off. I don’t mean difficulty, I have a hot take on that, I think there’s a middle point after you learn a rough idea of the game and the heroes l, when Dota immediately becomes easier than League, it becomes a matter of adding more skills to your repertoire, whereas VERY early in league you need to perfect what you already know since there isn’t really much to learn besides last hitting and champions. Perfecting is much more difficult, in league you’ll be playing against people who know what you know, you just need to squeeze another inch out to do better than them, which is much harder than how in Dota, in the early ranks so many people don’t know the base knowledge of a bunch of different mechanics the game has, which means you can learn how to do something (not even very well) and be a step ahead of most of the other players around your rank. 

Dota has real true freedom. In League, builds and playstyles funnel you into optimized paths, if you play this champion you build these items, and they are mostly the same items for every other champ in that role. In Dota, it feels like the game wants you to build whatever YOU want to do. Itemization isn’t just about stats, it’s about completely redefining your role. You can turn a support into a carry, a core into an initiator, or solve problems on the fly with creative purchases, oh, the enemy has a hero running through us but they are mostly basic attack based, get a heavens halberd which has an active that disarms the targeted enemy. It’s less about “what’s meta” and more about “what’s possible.”

And that philosophy extends to the heroes themselves. Every hero feels deeper, more complex, and more impactful. Abilities aren’t just skill shots or buffs—they reshape the battlefield. Take Broodmother, who doesn’t just move through the map, she claims it, spreading webs across the terrain until the battlefield becomes her domain, allowing her to skitter over cliffs and trees. Or Phoenix, who blazes through trees, destroying them instead of climbing over them. There’s also a day and night cycle where some heroes benefit while in one and others benefit while in the other. Some heroes have abilities that even change the map to day or night. Phoenix’s ult happens to be one of those, she turns herself into an egg releasing heat waves for 6 seconds and its day during those 6 seconds, when she breaks out she stuns enemies in the radius and is reborn with all non-ult cooldowns refreshed and full health and mana. 

Almost every hero has something like this—some unique, game-warping mechanic.

A great comparison point is Monkey King versus Wukong.

Wukong in League dashes in with Nimbus Strike, spins through enemies with Cyclone, and leaves behind a decoy to confuse opponents. Pretty damn basic. 

Monkey King, on the other hand. He doesn’t just fight on the battlefield, he ascends above it. With Tree Dance, he leaps from tree top to tree top, not even using mana and he can’t be seen by normal means while doing this. Then, when you find your target you slam down with Primal Spring. If someone does spot you and has means to destroy the tree you’re on then you take damage and are stunned for 3 seconds. And he has a slam attack with his staff like Wukong, but his is much further and enlarges like it does in real world folklore. Monkey King’s ult summons an army of spectral warriors in a big aoe that attack any nearby enemies, Monkey King can also repeatedly take the place of one of the warriors making himself indistinguishable from the others and teleporting around his domain. 2 of the most iconic items in Dota are Aghanims shard and scepter, they both either dramatically change an ability or add an entirely new ability so that a hero with 4 abilities now has 6. Monkey King’s shard makes his staff strike also apply primal spring (leaping from a tree top to slam down) except for he leaps from his staff at the end of the slam instead of leaping from a tree. And the scepter upgrade makes it so the spectral soldiers from his ult randomly spawn around him. All Heroes also have innate passives (not included in their abilities) that play a massive role in how you play said hero. Monkey King’s makes it so he can disguise himself as part of the environment and even move around while looking like a tree or something. 

That’s really the core of Dota 2: everything feels bigger, riskier, and more rewarding. The map is more interactive, the heroes are more expressive, and the decisions carry more weight. It’s really not harder to learn, there’s just MORE to learn.

Fair warning to new players, there’s no forfeit button, the matches on average are longer than leagues, normal matches have pretty bad matchmaking, but when you unlock ranked I think it has the most balanced matchmaking I’ve experienced in my life of gaming. It is an extremely rewarding game when you take the time to learn it.

review_text:
Took me a while to write this review but... I love Dota2! I've been playing a variety of MOBAs for a long time now, but this one is my favorite.

The skill floor is incredibly high. Unlike some other MOBAs that focus heavily on mastering individual characters, Dota2 is really about understanding timings, map awareness, decision making, itemization and hero synergies/counters. It honestly feels closer to a strategy game. Every action you choose to take gives small, incremental advantages, and over time those stack into a huge lead if you play correctly.

That said, you have to be okay with losing A LOT. For a long time, you’re going to feel like you’re terrible at the game. This is an accepted fact. Dota2 doesn’t really start to feel “good” (or fun for some) until things finally click, which can take hundreds of hours.

This is not a casual-friendly game. It’s not something you can pick up once every few months and expect to enjoy because you’ll likely just get steamrolled by players with 500+ hours. But if you stick with it, the payoff is incredible. Few games match the feeling of improvement when your knowledge, decision-making, and mechanics finally come together.

If you’re willing to invest the time, Dota2 is one of the most rewarding competitive experiences out there. Just be ready for the climb.

Also, I need more friends to play this with, so lets link up! :)

review_text: Obviously with this game being a MOBA, it is not for everyone. If you cannot handle the mental stress that comes alongside playing such a game, don't try it. To me, coming back to DotA 2 in 2026 feels pretty great. The menus are all fleshed out, the UI is sensible, you can look at things in the in-game wiki while MID MATCH. Skins exist in the form of individual pieces for characters, and that seems pretty cool. There are free battle passes with fully voice-acted stories and unique art. There's mastery and challenges for those who care, in-game guides made by players, different ways to play most of the cast. I'll sum my current experience up as: it can be a lot, and maybe too much at times. You will not know what you're doing until 500 hours or more into the game, probably. The game is balanced around everything being ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, and if you're someone who enjoys a more sandboxy/"free" feel to a game, I think you'll enjoy DotA 2.

review_text:
Dota 2 is a legendary game, but its refusal to include a proper forfeit button is one of its most outdated and frustrating design choices. In 2026, it feels absurd that players are still forced to sit through hopeless matches with no official way to surrender. When a game is clearly lost — your lanes are crushed, your map is dark, and your team morale is gone — being trapped for another 15 to 25 minutes isn’t “competitive integrity.” It’s just a waste of time.

Instead of giving players a clean, structured surrender option like other modern MOBAs, Dota 2 pushes people toward toxic behavior: AFKing, griefing, or abandoning. None of these outcomes improve the match quality or the community. They simply punish players who want to move on and play a fresh game.

Valve often justifies this by pointing to Dota’s comeback potential, but let’s be honest — not every match is a miracle story. Sometimes a stomp is just a stomp. A forfeit vote wouldn’t kill the spirit of the game; it would respect players’ time and reduce negativity.

Dota 2 is brilliant, but this missing feature makes the experience feel unnecessarily punishing, outdated, and player‑unfriendly.

review_text:
I am happy to have played during the "golden age". The back and forth of China and the west trading TI wins, battle passes, peak player numbers and the big 7.0 Monkey King major revamp patches. 

Over the years, Dota had to slow down, newer generation of players have much too many options for free games, let alone paid ones. When I started playing games, it was either Dota, league of CS, everything else did not have a big enough player base.

Point being, this is easily one of the best video games and projects of all time. It is crazy to me that after all this time, I still am such a big fan, some heroes I can play and never get bored of. While other people say this game is unbalanced or not updated enough, those people are delusional. As far as a letter grade is concerned, Valve deserves an S, to keep the game as close to the original vision while making it interesting enough and using the corporate side to graduate Dota from a silly map mod to a 40million dollar event year on year, that is not something that just happens. 

Ultimately, Dota is in it's sunset years, while the game wont die, there needs to be a fresh set of legs with something as big as an 8.0 and a newer generation of players.

review_text:
Dota 2 是一款免费、公平、深度无底洞的硬核 MOBA，没有 Pay-to-Win，所有英雄免费，皮肤只改外观不改平衡，这一点在现在的网游里非常难得。
最大的魅力就是深度与自由度。120+ 个英雄、上百件装备，没有固定套路，同一个英雄能打出完全不同的玩法。每一局都是全新的博弈，对线、发育、视野、团战、战术拉扯，脑力与操作双重考验，永远有新东西可以学，玩几千小时也只是入门。
7.39 大更新后体验好了很多：商店优化、自动预购、信使自动运装备、买活信息更清晰，对新手友好了不少。画面和特效也在一直升级，虽然不算最顶尖，但耐看、辨识度高，低配电脑也能流畅运行。
电竞生态更是顶级：国际邀请赛（TI）奖金池曾经是全球最高，职业比赛观赏性拉满，战术与个人操作的巅峰对决，看比赛也是一种巨大乐趣。
缺点也很明显：
新手劝退门槛极高：机制复杂、英雄多、术语多、没人带很难坚持下去。
社区环境一般：喷子、挂机、消极行为不少，单排心态容易炸。
版本节奏快：经常大改，老玩家也要不断学习新套路。
单排体验不稳定：匹配系统有时不靠谱，输赢很看队友。
但如果你能跨过新手期、找到固定开黑好友，Dota 2 会是你玩得最久、最难忘的游戏。它不讨好轻度玩家，只为真正喜欢深度竞技、团队配合、无限可能性的人而生。
推荐度：9/10，强烈推荐给硬核 MOBA 爱好者。它不是最好上手的游戏，但绝对是最有深度、最公平、最值得长期投入的 MOBA。

review_text:
Dota 2 is easily one of the deepest and most competitive games ever made. The strategy, teamwork, and skill ceiling are insane. But at the same time, it can be frustrating, toxic, and very hard to get into.

🎮 Gameplay & Depth:
✔ Massive hero pool — each hero feels completely unique
✔ Strategy is next-level — items, timing, positioning, teamwork all matter
✔ Every match feels different — no fixed pattern
✔ Skill ceiling is extremely high — always something to improve

🧠 Learning Curve:
✖ Very hard for beginners — you will struggle at the start
✖ Requires game knowledge, map awareness, and teamwork
✖ Takes time (a lot of time) to get good

😤 Problems:
✖ Community can be toxic — especially in ranked matches
✖ Matches are long — not good if you want quick games
✖ Can feel stressful and mentally draining

🔥 Overall Experience:
Dota 2 is not a casual game. It’s serious, competitive, and demanding. If you commit time and learn it properly, it becomes one of the most rewarding games ever. But if you’re not patient, you’ll hate it.

👉 Honest recommendation:
If you want a deep, skill-based game — play it.
If you want chill, easy fun — stay away.

review_text:
I honestly don’t know where to begin with *Dota 2*, other than to say uninstalling it has been one of the best decisions I’ve made in a long time.

The matchmaking is an absolute mess. Games feel predetermined half the time — you either get completely stomped with zero chance of winning, or you roll over the enemy team in 15 minutes with no resistance. There’s no sense of balance, no real competition, just a frustrating cycle of extremes that makes every match feel pointless.

Then there’s the community. Toxic doesn’t even begin to describe it. Every game feels like walking into a verbal warzone, where blame, insults, and griefing are the norm rather than the exception. Instead of teamwork, you get hostility. Instead of strategy, you get arguments.

The cheating and smurfing situation makes everything even worse. It genuinely feels like there are smurfs or cheaters in almost every game, completely ruining any chance of fair play. When you can’t trust the integrity of matches, what’s even the point of playing?

And don’t get me started on the UI. It’s cluttered, bloated, and constantly pushing items and market-related junk in your face. It feels less like a game interface and more like a storefront with a game awkwardly built around it.

At the end of the day, the experience is just exhausting. Between broken matchmaking, a toxic player base, and the constant presence of smurfs and cheaters, the game stops being fun entirely.

Not playing this garbage is genuinely one of the best things you can do with your time.

review_text:
Cyberpunk 2077 is not really about becoming the best mercenary in Night City.

It is about personhood under siege.

It is science fiction, but only by degree. People are already flattened into profiles, performances, labour value, social image, sexual currency, and marketable identity. Cyberpunk 2077 just pushes that process until it becomes impossible to ignore.

Its cruelest idea is simple:
you are born hoping to become someone real, and the world immediately starts negotiating what parts of you it can own.

Johnny Silverhand is what makes that idea personal. He is rage, regret, defiance, and the voice that refuses to let compromise feel normal. The part of you that would rather burn than disappear quietly into something obedient and useful.

The journey is about trying to stay a person in a world that wants a product.

And even then, it still leaves room for one stubborn answer:

No.

The turnaround is staggering. They fixed virtually everything people complained about at release, and what is left now is a masterpiece. Story, gameplay, visuals, aesthetic, atmosphere, music - 10/10 across the board.

Very few games, films, or books have hit me this hard in years. It has been two weeks since I finished it, and it still has not left my head. I am still listening to songs from the in-game radio and thinking about Night City, its people, and everything this game made me feel. That alone says more than any score I could give it.

review_text:
[h3]There are no happy endings - just different ways to lose[/h3]

Solid game, little unpolished at times, but with lots of attention given to the world.
Some interesting perk trees and cyberware to change up how you engage with combat and traversal.

Hard to play this game without making some comparisons to GTA5 and one thing that stood out to me was the AI.
The cops are easy to lose and other enemies are equally easy to bug out and cheese. I wish more imagination was used when designing the criminal system here.

Voice acting and visuals are at a high standard and the dialogue/writing is mostly decent, with some amazing highs at certain points in the story.
Similar with the soundtrack, its decent but there's a few absolute bangers mixed in there too.

After finishing the story with multiple endings, i feel emotionally drained, but im still craving more. 
It's one of those rare 'must play' games you only get a few times each generation.

⠀

review_text:
This still remains one of my favorite games to chill in. When other games aren't quite hitting, I come to the beautiful (but brutal) Night City.

Every system I've played on, I've owned Cyberpunk. PS, XBX, GOG and now Steam. It never gets boring because each playthrough is different thanks to the variety of builds. 

And honestly, they're all fun to play as. I enjoy being a Netrunner and taking out whole rooms of enemies without stepping into it just as much as I enjoy double jumping over a fence, dashing in the air and headshotting someone before my feet touch the ground. 

- If you enjoy great stories in your games, this has it. 
- If you enjoy great gameplay in your games, this has it. 
- If you enjoy looking at great graphics, this remains the benchmark for that. Especially with path tracing enabled.

And if you enjoy an immersive world to be in, something to escape the real world, with amazing ambience, this is as good as it gets and will probably be that for me until the day the sequel comes out.

review_text:
The kind of stupid fake RPG people that don't actually like RPGs like. Terrible RPG mechanics with big skill trees that just change very little. This is a game with cybernetic implants yet there's so little ability to meaningfully change or augment your character. So much is just % stat modifiers and it's all so bland and creatively bankrupt.

The game creates a narrative of urgency, only to drop you into an open world encouraging you to waste as much time as possible in Night City. This kind of gameplay-narrative dissonance really takes away from the storytelling, in my opinion. The story itself seems decent enough, there's good voice acting and facial animations, but the game refuses to break first person which can make the storytelling feel stiff and limited by this decision. I find it hard to immerse when protagonist V just feels like a camera & voicebox watching people talk rather than a living part of the world.

Combat is very bland & repetitive and there is just so much of it to get through. Driving is the absolute basic level of serviceable. Character customisation is okay, but again it's largely first-person so it feels a lot like fluff. Music was really not for me. Graphics are mostly great though.

This is a crowd-pleasing 'RPG' for those that just want to mindlessly kill things and experience a drawn out story, but for me it all just feels so empty and soulless despite trying to like it.

review_text:
This game pisses me off. This [i]should[/i] be a masterpiece, if not for one egregious flaw. The combat is terrible, specifically the enemy AI. NPCs are Skyrim levels of stupid, and difficulty means bigger health bar. That is genuinely what 90% of combat encounters in vanilla offer you. There is no dynamism to all but a few select boss encounters. By the time I started getting tier 4 weapons I was basically just god, running around instantly ending everyone as I saw fit; and I played the entire campaign on hard. The combat is so ass it actually dissuaded me from exploring. Typically in games like this I like to explore every nook and cranny I can find, but in this one I thought what is the point? I'm just going to instantly end any fight I get into and the rewards are meaningless crafting supplies I already have 10,000 of and have never needed to use ever.

Everything else about this game is amazing. The soundtrack, the city, the visuals, the driving (the only game to make me avoid using fast travel because driving around is really just that fun), all of it is great, except for the archaic Gamebryo-ass NPC AI. There are a lot of other nitpicks I could get into but none of it is as game-breaking as the terrible combat system. There are so many cool quickhacks and weapons you can use and none of it matters because you just gun down everyone instantly. I ended up skipping the DLC because I was so bored of the same combat over and over and over. 

All of that being said, there is still plenty of enjoyment to get out of this game, it just disappoints me that they screwed up such a fundamental aspect of gameplay so badly that it kind of ruined the game for me.

Worth it on a heavy discount, but anything more and I'd say wait. You're not missing out on a whole lot besides atmosphere and story, even if they are the best atmosphere and story I've experienced in an FPS.

I enjoyed this game far more than I thought I would. I knew very little about it, only a passing knowledge that it released to a poor reception because of bugs and poor optimization.

I however got to experience this on a powerful setup with all the RTX sexiness turned up to ultra, with a lot of the issues fixed. Beautiful game and one of the most detailed cities I have ever seen. Strong story and writing that I became very invested in. Very good voice performances from the main cast...even Keanu, who, whilst can sometimes sound a bit wooden, he gives an impassioned performance here. Also this is one of the few open world games where I did not want to fast travel...I enjoyed driving through the gorgeous Night City.

However, I do have some issues. The city LOOKS great, but feels quite dead at times. If you stop to smell the roses, you'll see NPCs silently repeating animations and walking back and forth like robots. You can't interact with anything really either.

Also I did run in to a few bugs...especially in the DLC Phantom Liberty. One or two game breaking bugs where I actually had to roll back saves, losing some progress.

review_text:
The most non-plussed 50 hours of my life. I don't even know where the time went. The entire game feels like a half-remembered dream.

Let me start with the most obvious implication on gameplay: the user interface. It is so overloaded with numbers and colors and flashing icons. It never feels familiar. I'm 50 hours into the game and trying to remember what button gets me to exit a car. When combat happens there are so many bars and statuses and timers and whatever else... I have no idea what is going on. All of this obscures the fact that pretty much every encounter I take out an SMG, empty it into a dude, and he dies. If take too much damage, I dash around a corner and press the heal button. I don't say this to be reductive. I say this to say: for all the complex user interface decisions, it's not a complicated game mechanically.

The next most-immediate part of the experience is the story. Maybe more specifically the lore. I felt like I was drowning for the prologue. To the game's credit, this isn't really an important part of the game. It doesn't really start until Keanu Reeves shows up and after that it is mostly coherent. I think the peak was in the desert with Panam and her crew where there was a fairly straight forward story with some personals takes. A little bit of the Arasaka storyline with that one due who helps you. That made sense to me. Immediately after that... I have no idea what happened. I'm on the literal last mission -- where it tells you "this is the point of no return" -- and I have no idea what is happening in this story.

What drove me to review this game — after 50 hours and slowly going through it for more than a year — was the unbelievably insulting side quest design. This extends to Phantom Liberty too. Every side quest — and I really mean EVERY SINGLE SIDE QUEST — is not designed to be played, it is designed to be watched. This game has more than 10-20 hours of standing there while someone talks at you. It is unbelievable how boring this game can be.

I have been agonizing finishing this game. There was a part of me that enjoyed it enough to buy the expansion and make it through 50 hours worth of content. Then at some point I must have ran out of the game and all that was left was cut scenes and dialogue. I have attempted to play this game 4 times over the past 4 weeks. All 4 sessions were 1 hour to 90 minutes of watching characters talk at me. And it's not even interesting! It's not science fiction concepts. It's not moral quandaries. It's not interesting characters. It's just YAPPING. Talking and talking and talking and talking. 

I've had a rocky relatiomship with this game. I bought it on release day and it was in such a bad state I uninstalled it and never picked it up again for 4 years. I finally got back into it in late December 2024 and over the course of 2025 I logged the hours you see here now. At some point, I couldn't do it anymore. I've stretched this out over several months now but I've accepted I have gotten all the value there is in this game. It is so unbelievably boring.

review_text:
Cyberpunk is an absolute blast. Very well executed story, emotional moments and very engaging. Visually stunning even with a powerful but not gaming laptop. I've already started a second playthrough, taking things a lot slower this time.

If you like sci-fi near-future environments and RPGs you'll really enjoy it. It's not deepest RPG ever, it's not the longest game either, but everything that is packed in here is so worth it.

A few negatives:
- The side content is nowhere near as well executed as the main story. All the parts are there, but you have to read a lot more to get what is going on.
- The vehicle handling is very meh, I don't really enjoy it very much
- The AI can be a bit janky

Overall though this is a great journey through a visually stunning world. What it lacks in depth it makes up for in character.

review_text:
I put off on buying this game for the longest time after hearing about how broken and under-baked it was on launch. And boy, it must have been something atrocious, because even now after what I can only assume has been dozens of major fixes and additions, it is still a buggy mess. Within the first 30 minutes of the game, I managed to get myself stuck in the player stash room because for some reason if you close the door after entering the room there is no way to open the door again. After leaving the apartment, a woman sitting with her back to the balcony stood up, warped upwards onto the railing, turned around and walked off and plumeted 60 feet to her death. I would often see, in one area, 4-5 NPCs with the exact same model only to turn around 180 degrees and see another group all sporting a different identical model. It was so bad, in fact, I was impressed if I was able to look somewhere other than the sky and NOT see at least two identical models in my field of view. I couldn't walk 20 feet without seeing broken animations. A plaza with 5 people all sitting down with their arms raised above their heads like they're being held at gunpoint, but conversing with each other completely normally. I can't count on the fingers I have how many people were managing to smoke with the cigarette only ever coming within 6 inches of their mouth.

Maybe the rest of the story is good. Maybe the gameplay is good. But I wouldn't know, because 4 hours into the game I am still in what appears to be the most boring and context deprived prologue I've ever experienced, and I can't even save myself by just deciding to free roam because you're restricted to a roughly 2 square foot area of the city during this time. If you decide to venture outside that area, too bad, you get a message on the screen that literally says "there's nothing out there for you", your screen goes black, and your car magically flips around.

Well, there's nothing in here for me either.

review_text:
Generally, I loved this game.
The world building, different atmosphere of its districts, the presentation and gameplay were all incredibly well done. I got rather addicted to the gameplay loop I would find myself in. Once you find your preferred playstyle, it unlocks all the great things in this game. The gunplay and stealth were well balanced. The movement is rather smooth.  The story is compelling as are many side quests.
I would also say, the vehicle handling is amazing. Most every vehicle you can get feels different in their own way. Turning was not always easy, but felt great. I could see a lot of potential if CD Project decided to go back to this kind of game, Id be incredibly interested right up front. 
Going through the Phantom Liberty dlc, I found that more compelling (and possibly the ending) than the core game.

For some critique: It never really felt as deep with its systems as Witcher 3, but that is an unfair comparison. Totally different types of games. I complimented the gameplay earlier, but I fell into "my way of gameplay" early on. And from reading descriptions of augmentations one can purchase for their upgrades, I never felt compelled to branch out and try new things.  
The world of Night City does feel huge, but in a container. I never felt small. But that may be in a way that you are limited to ground traversal at all times.
 The voice acting is up and down, and will admit to playing male V, and found the voice acting grating. The accent continuously through me off. Since I was playing with Phantom Liberty, the quality and accents of certain characters change often. 

A worthy get for a game. I got mine on sale. Id recommend  that if you want the complete game.

As a player once said: when you download this game, you step into a virtual city where you can do what you want and immerse yourself in its characters.
Night City glows with neon, where light and shadow blur together, and I’m just an ordinary person being pushed forward through loss and struggle.

In the end, you’re not just playing a game—you’re experiencing a AAA world that truly connects with your emotions.

review_text:
"The Greatest Comeback in Gaming History"

It is 2026, and I can finally say that Cyberpunk 2077 is the game we were promised back in 2020—and then some. If you were holding off because of the launch drama, the "Redemption Arc" is officially complete. This is now a mandatory experience for anyone who loves RPGs or immersive sims.

Night City & Atmosphere
The city itself is the main character. It is the most dense, visually stunning, and atmospheric open world ever built. Now that the technical issues are ironed out and PS5 Pro/high-end PC support has peaked, walking through the neon-soaked streets of Japantown or the lawless slums of Dogtown is breathtaking. It finally feels "alive."

Build Variety & Combat (The RPG Core)
The 2.0 and 2.1 overhauls changed everything. Combat isn't just about shooting; it's about Synergy:

The Cyberdeck/Netrunner Build: You can clear entire rooms without firing a shot by jumping from camera to camera, uploading quickhacks.

The "Sandevistan" Ninja: Using slow-mo tech to deflect bullets with a katana feels incredible.

The Synergy: Just like a good TD game, you can mix and match. Combine poison-tipped knives with hacks that spread on death, or use high-explosive arms with perks that make you immune to your own blasts. The skill trees are finally deep enough to reward creative playstyles.

Phantom Liberty & Story
The Phantom Liberty expansion is the gold standard for DLC. It adds a spy-thriller vibe with Idris Elba that is arguably better than the main quest. The writing is mature, the choices actually feel heavy, and the emotional payoff is brutal in the best way possible.

Performance Note
Unlike the early days, the game is now remarkably stable. It's no longer a "resource hog" for no reason; the optimization is top-tier. Even on mid-range hardware, you can get a smooth, beautiful experience.

Pros:

Impeccable Storytelling: Top-tier voice acting and cinematic direction.

True Build Freedom: Every perk feels like it adds a new way to play.

Visual Masterpiece: Still the benchmark for "Next Gen" graphics in 2026.

Cons:

Initial Grind: The first few hours have a lot of dialogue and setup (though the payoff is worth it).

Driving: While improved, the vehicle physics can still feel a bit "floaty" compared to dedicated racing games.

Final Verdict
Night City eventually wins, but the journey to the top is the best 100+ hours you can spend in a digital world. Don't skip this one.

review_text:
First time playing Cyberpunk? Buy it, install it, and prepare to go on a trip. 

This game blends stealth, technical, and even guns blazing gameplay incredibly well. I ran around pinging "Short Circuit" on everyone on Normal Difficulty, but the game REALLY got fleshed out when I went on the harder difficulties. Still, I recommend "Normal" if it's your first time as a merc in Night City. Go ham.

Returning to Cyberpunk? Reinstall it, and play it on "Hard" or "VERY HARD". Mods optional but recommended.

Like your other "build" games like Fallout, the higher the difficulty the more important it is to spec into a build. It makes progression feel significantly better as your cyberware really does build up, and the game WILL kick your butt until you reach that stage. Become a Netrunner that kills before anyone else sees you, or a Desperado from Metal Gear Rising with some heavy & sharp Mantis Blades.

For example, I set out for a "Gorilla Arms" build and at first I had to draw out Cyberpsychos from their den so I could run them over with my car. I went from just grabbing whatever gun could make me that money I needed for those sweet new implants to going full Donkey Kong on some mad fools, throwing their comrades around like barrels off a crimson girder.

Either way, be ready to hit the big leagues, choom. And be sure to pay back Victor.

review_text:
If you like 3 or more of the following things in any game then get CP2077...

- Fluid gameplay with plenty of options to perfectly suit your style of playing
- RPG mechanics that are fun and rewarding
- Great graphics and amazing Art-Direction
- An immersive open world
- An engaging storyline that isn't overdone just for the sake of content, but...
- ...a good number of sidequests that are well written and NOT of the fetching kind
- on top of that sidequests that may surprise you
- believable characters and some really heartfelt moments
- Great Art-Direction and Art-Design
- the most amazing gunplay there is
- replayability just because the gameplay is great
- great cut-scenes
- surprisingly fun vehicle-physics for a game of that kind
- tense moments
- colours

I really dislike words like "masterpiece" or "one-of-a-kind" and you are to decide if CP2077 is one of those to you... But to me that game is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ masterpiece that sticks with you even after the credits rolled.

And to add to this: This is the first game that made me write a review on steam. That's how much I like it. '

So? What are you waiting for?

review_text:
I decided to wait a while after the initial release to let the developers fix the launch issues and give them some time to polish the game (yep, 5 years). Yet, all these years later, I’m still running into various glitches that are definitely not "gameplay features" or "implant malfunctions."

- [b]visual glitches[/b]: blinking shadows, dissolving cars, Path Tracing issues with strobing lights, and jittering textures.
- [b]soft-locks[/b]: several bugs in different quests that force you to restart from the last checkpoint.

I really wish there was an option to save while in elevators or during scripted scenes, but alas.

By the way, this isn’t an RPG in the classical sense. I mean, it’s a bad sign when the dialogue system in Fallout 4 is fundamentally better (which is hilarious in itself). This is a [i] "new school" RPG[/i], where the role-playing element is mostly limited to picking your skill tree.

That said, the game does have a decent and, at times, high-octane story (even if it is mostly "on rails" and follows a character who consistently makes stupid decisions). It also boasts a great soundtrack (I’m talking about the original score, not the radio stations) and, of course, Keanu Reeves.

review_text:
I didn't come to save it. I came to survive. But in the end, I realized that in this city, you either die a legend or live long enough to become a ghost.

Cyberpunk 2077 isn't the game they promised at launch. Forget that noise. This is what it became after 2.0 and Phantom Liberty: a scarred, brutal, multi-layered work of art where every neon reflection feels like a memory you haven't made yet.

I didn't just finish this story. I lived it. Every walk through the rain in Dogtown, every Samurai riff on the radio, every painful choice with Panam, Judy, or Johnny—it sticks with you. Idris Elba is an absolute menace (play his path). The characters aren't broken; they're just real.

If you want a brainless open-world shooter, move on. This game gets under your skin and asks: "If today was your last day, how do you want to be remembered?"

10/10 for storytelling and pure, gritty soul.
For Johnny. For Judy. For Panam. For V. And for every single spark that refused to die in this beautiful, terrible city.

review_text:
Cyberpunk 2077 does a great job making its world feel real. Night City isn’t just there to look good, it actually feels alive when you’re moving around in it. Driving, walking, random encounters… it all flows naturally and doesn’t feel like you’re just going from marker to marker.

Side jobs and gigs are one of the best parts of the game. They’re quick to get into, don’t waste your time, and most of them are actually fun to play. It never really feels like filler content, which is rare for an open world game.

There’s also a lot of freedom in how you play. You can go full stealth, play as a netrunner, build a tanky character, or mix things up however you want. Different builds actually feel different, not just stat changes.

Story-wise, both the main game and Phantom Liberty are just absolute cinema. The overall story quality is really high, and the characters are memorable in a way most games don’t manage.

review_text:
The kind of stupid fake RPG people that don't actually like RPGs like. Terrible RPG mechanics with big skill trees that just change very little. This is a game with cybernetic implants yet there's so little ability to meaningfully change or augment your character. So much is just % stat modifiers and it's all so bland and creatively bankrupt.

The game creates a narrative of urgency, only to drop you into an open world encouraging you to waste as much time as possible in Night City. This kind of gameplay-narrative dissonance really takes away from the storytelling, in my opinion. The story itself seems decent enough, there's good voice acting and facial animations, but the game refuses to break first person which can make the storytelling feel stiff and limited by this decision. I find it hard to immerse when protagonist V just feels like a camera & voicebox watching people talk rather than a living part of the world.

Combat is very bland & repetitive and there is just so much of it to get through. Driving is the absolute basic level of serviceable. Character customisation is okay, but again it's largely first-person so it feels a lot like fluff. Music was really not for me. Graphics are mostly great though.

This is a crowd-pleasing 'RPG' for those that just want to mindlessly kill things and experience a drawn out story, but for me it all just feels so empty and soulless despite trying to like it.

review_text: Growing up I always wanted to play an open-world game that felt convincing enough, that I could really get into character. For me, Cyberpunk 2077 is the first time I truly experienced that. I know not everyone loves how combat feels, but to me, the story richness and atmosphere is the strength of the game. I enjoy driving to my destinations, getting distracted by things and listening to the radio; I enjoy the slow passage of time and pretty to-scale map distances -- they make the world and my time playing feel "full". I'm not an FPS gamer, and I struggle with shooting games but to me, combat feels manageable at the Hard difficulty, and there are many ways to approach each scenario you may land yourself in, which really helps me not feel railroaded at any given point, and contributes to the game as a roleplaying experience. If this type of reasoning makes sense to you, get the game. You'll probably enjoy it as much as I did.

review_text:
Cyberpunk 2077 is one of the best games ever made. The story is truly unique and you really feel for the characters. The voice actors did a fantastic job especially male V. While some prefer the female performance, I found the male voice to be incredibly intimidating and impactful.

The gameplay is top-notch. You can play differently every single run, and even repeating a build never feels boring. However, even with the freedom of the skill tree, some dialogue options or outcomes are blocked by the level cap. Even with mods, you can’t max out everything, which is one of the game's few downsides.

Night City feels unique. Even without the random world events found in games like Red Dead Redemption 2, the side gigs provide plenty of content, offering at least 40 hours of playtime on a first run. The endings are also stellar; each one is distinct and worth experiencing.

The romance options feel genuine because they require significant investment unlike The Witcher 3, where some encounters feel more random. Furthermore, the Phantom Liberty DLC improves on the base game's missions significantly. It’s clear CDPR wanted to create the best expansion possible.

If you have a decent rig, this game is absolutely worth buying. It remains one of the best-looking titles on the market and a staple for hardware benchmarks.

10/10

review_text:
I waited to write this review to let time pass, observe mechanical updates, and distance myself from real-world events I experienced during my first playthrough. My conclusions remain unchanged. I played on release via Stadia (logging around 600 hours, bug-free) and later transitioned to Steam with the expansion. 

If you are looking for a highly structured, immersive world where you can exist as a single character, this game delivers. But beneath the surface, it is a heavy, uncompromising examination of autonomy, mental health, and the ethical cost of survival.

## Fair content warning: ##  This review contains spoilers and discusses severe mental health issues and suicide.

## THE WORLD AND THE ATMOSPHERE

As someone who grew up in the 80s, the retro-future aesthetic resonates well with me. The environmental design — ranging from corporate skyscrapers to neon-lit, trash-filled alleys — balances surface splendour with the tragedy of an unforgiving dystopia. The soundscape is very good, and the OST remains in my regular rotation. I particularly liked the diegetic inclusion of music through radios, speakers, etc. It feels natural. 

The developers successfully created a graphically believable universe with its own distinct clothing, colloquialisms, and professions.

On the downside, I wish they had done something like the procedural, emergent roleplay of games like *Daggerfall*. The city is dense, but the NPCs lack complex schedules or interactions. Most of the world is setting, and there are few things (or people!) you can interact with. It was a satisfying (but frustrating) realization to find out you could actually confess yourself in the absolution vending machines.

There is also a distinct tension  — and this is a crucial issue  — between exploring the world and the main quest. The narrative insists V is dying rapidly, urging you to rush. It makes absolute sense, but doing so deprives you of exploring the setting. But if you suspend the dissonance this urgency creates, the lore and visual storytelling are highly detailed.

## GAMEPLAY AND MECHANICS

The gunplay and movement mechanics are satisfying, offering varied approaches: fast-paced reflexes, time-dilating cyberware, or real-time quickhacks. The combat functions well, accommodating everything from stealth to direct assault.

However, the RPG elements lack structural consequence. The reputation system is shallow: working for one gang should logically anger others, and aiding the police should make you a target in the underworld. This all could lead tor restricting access to certain resources or quests based on alliances, and would foster replayability.

Similarly, the game allows complete character respecs. I value games where you can make your own choices, but choices must have some degree of consequence, and this applies also to character creation and development. A system where choices have permanence — where mastering hacking precludes being a swordmaster, for instance — would ground the experience. 

The game also omits cyberpsychosis as a gameplay mechanic for the player. Lore dictates that heavy bodily modification risks mental breakdown, yet the game imposes no such penalty on V. This is ironic because cyberpsychosis is hardly forgotten in these modern iterations of the setting: the entire Edgerunners series runs on it. It is also (in the TTRPG) a very important mechanic to limit the possibilitites of expanding your own character or respeccing it.

These issues I am critiquing are not a problem for Cyberpunk 2077 only — this plague is an AAA development philosophy hesitant to lock players out of content, but in doing so, robs them of actual consequence to their actions.

## NARRATIVE, MENTAL HEALTH, AND ENDINGS

Yet, apart from my appreciation of its lore and aesthetics, where the game holds the most value for me is in its somber handling of psychological trauma and survival. I have seen other players too credit this game's themes with helping them process dark times in their own lives, and looking at the quests, one can imagine why. 

The game repeatedly forces engagement with autonomy and personhood: The Delamain Arc requires permanent choices regarding a fractured AI's perception of reality. Brendan is vending machine treated as corporate property — he insists he is not sentient, yet acts as if he is, raising practical questions about the definition of personhood. Lizzy Wizzy is clearly suffering from cyberpsychosis, but also facing the horror of having her mind tampered with by a partner. Barry's story is an approach to depression from a darkly humorous angle, but delivers a grim look at how mental health struggles are ignored by peers.

Cyberpsycho sightings themselves, on which I haven't seen much comment, are excuses for boss fights, ok. But if you look through them, they are tragedies. The one titled 'Family Matters' hit very hard, with one woman struggling to help her sister, and ultimately losing her husband and even her life to it.

People often cite that the main narrative is 'V is dying', but that isn't accurate: the body can survive. V is facing her mind being rewritten by the Relic, and that is a much more horrible reality. I think the game does a good job at presenting this, both subtly and explicitly: V's dialogue options and actions (like smoking) begin to mirror Johnny Silverhand, and Johnny's own arc shifts as he realizes this, moving from hostility to empathy. You are fading away, and the game acknowledges the terror of that process.

## CONTENT WARNING: SUICIDE DISCUSSION

During my first playthrough, I was dealing with the immediate aftermath of a suicide attempt by someone close to me. I interrupted the attempt, but witnessing their intense pain left me grappling with whether I did them a disservice. I prioritize personal autonomy, and intervening felt like an intrusion on their ultimate choice, even if it stemmed from a compromised mental state.

Processing the game through that lens influenced how I saw the final choices. Players often view the endings as a way to «win» but, to me, they were a calculation of whom I was willing to sacrifice. Asking the Nomads for help gets many of them killed to buy you time. Siding with Rogue gets her killed. Siding with Arasaka surrenders both your mind and Johny's to them. The secret «solo» ending requires the mass murder of corporate guards who are ultimately just working people.

Under this light, the suicide ending seems the cleanest, most ethical option. It respects the autonomy and safety of everyone else. You prioritize your friends' lives above your survival, deciding not to drag them into your self-destruction. Johnny respects the choice. Your friends will call in the credits, grieving and cursing you, but the structural fact remains: you did not force anyone else to pay the price for your life.

## CONCLUSION

I acknowledge my experience with this game comes from a very specific, difficult place in my life, which colored my perception. Mechanically, it has flaws and missed opportunities for deeper RPG consequences. However, it captures the genre's aesthetic accurately and delivers a solid narrative. If you are willing to engage with its themes of autonomy, mental health, and the systemic cost of survival, I think it offers a dense, worthwhile experience.

review_text:
My personal gripes for disliking the game are as follows: The way it is designed means you can do any kind of run or build an all around character and let me tell you the content and world building is to die for. But it begins to fall flat when there is not much variety in weapons due to them just being scaled into tiers of 1-5. This was a great RPG to play but there is no pacing with the side quests and a lot of the purchases you make can be avoided and you can just focus on getting Cybernetic augments and that becomes the game as become and evolved lifeform. You can ignore hacking, you can ignore melee combat you can ignore being a stealth build. Which is fine and proper but once you find a gameplay style that works for you the entire game stops feeling fun and its just a "go here and do this, kill x amount of people, get paid."

Still one hell of a story, one I admire but you see the flaws. V's story is one where there are no good endings only what you willing to sacrifice to go big or die in an unmarked grave. Once you put speed bumps on the story I find you can easily break this the perk systems so your playstyle moves from FPS to mass murdering simulation. Good if you want that but I honestly wish I knew more going into the Cyberpunk universe. Its a very old RPG and the California setting really did not to it must justice if you ask me.

I adored the characters and the journey. Hell I almost 200 hours into it. But its so good it misses out in other areas and many of the character customization features are designed for looks not for real immersion. Sure its got quality but to me its just another grind fest where you can ignore all the side quests and till have a fine time. That is a red flag for me. I have always been a fan of CD Projekt Red but trying to do a dystopian rogue AI landscape works except there are no monsters and the ruins of civilization for a corporate supremacy becomes a boring environment quickly. I felt drained and disappointed after completing the main missions in both Vanilla and Phantom Liberty because nothing improves in the world its just you signing off permanetly what is a well written world where technology replaces a good degree of mythology but so much is missing.

I would have loved to see real mutants or hybrids which do exist lore wise in the game but again, so much time and money was spent on making a high fi retro vibe for all designs and culture you see inside of 2077 it removes everything else. You remain a boring human with boring yet flashy character designs or customizations to your body but they really do nothing. You are in a vice grip of conflict that coexists such interesting character story arc's that once you realize not many of them end well the experience becomes draining but a ride worth taking once. Repeated playthroughs with making different choices offer some thrill and then its gone. Violently, you are left looking for the next thrill, firefight or something you didn't discover. Like the desert of the Mohave, this place dries you up fast. 

You are in for a wild ride but don't expect any rewards long term. Feels good to become a badass and literally become the center of attention but how that plays into exploring anything beyond that? Its a hollow shell like those drained oil fields or trash heaps, and the world becomes very boring. I give them a 9/10 but personally the more I play and beat the game over and over again, the more I dislike 2077 with a heavy heart. I would have liked to see a game modeled after the original source material this RPG was molded by decades ago. But alas, my purist pursuits of old world RPGs and their iconic names means this game could never have lived long to my expectations. 

Still I was fine paying 60$ for it, the quality and content is there but once you milk it all.....don't expect it to give anything back with interest. I am happy to say now I know what the hype is about. CD Projekt Red can make a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ killer story which remains emotionally charged and damaging. I just wish there was more options and variety. I don't hate this game but I expected more when it came to roleplaying. This plays like "Doomplaying" given how your main character is running out of time regardless of what happens.

A flawed game that still delivers one of the most memorable journeys in modern RPGs.
